reasoning: The target federal funds range is expected to remain unchanged between the November 9, 2025 forecast date and the first two resolution dates (Nov 16 and Dec 9), as there are no scheduled FOMC meetings in that inter-meeting period. The only way the effective federal funds rate (EFFR) would strictly increase on these dates compared to Nov 9 is through day-to-day market micro-fluctuations (e.g. +1 or +2 basis points), perhaps driven by reserve scarcity generated by ongoing Quantitative Tightening (QT). These upward drifts happen but are relatively rare, as EFFR tends to flatline near its typical spread to the interest on reserve balances (IORB). By the third resolution date (Feb 7, 2026), the FOMC will have held their December 2025 and January 2026 meetings. While the overarching policy trend in 2025 has been an easing cycle (cutting rates down to around 4% by late Q3/Q4), there is meaningful uncertainty regarding potential inflation resurgence or economic resilience which could force a pause or even a rate hike. Abiding by the forecasting discipline that uncertainty scales with horizon, the probability for the final date shifts closer to 0.5, reflecting this widening set of macroeconomic possibilities over a multi-month period.
